one_line_takeaway: A single-author formal-methods framework that reframes agent-skill supply-chain defense as sound capability verification (F1 96.95%, 0 false positives) rather than heuristic scanning -- theoretically appealing and fast, but evaluated only on a synthetically generated benchmark with no head-to-head baseline and with registry-dependent attacks (dependency confusion 0%, typosquatting 50%) and runtime enforcement left unaddressed.
key_finding: On SkillFortifyBench (540 skills; Table 4) the tool reports F1 96.95%, precision 100% (254/254), recall 94.07% (254/270), accuracy 97.04% (524/540), with TP=254, FP=0, TN=270, FN=16 -- i.e., zero false positives on 270 benign skills (95% Wilson CI [0.0%, 1.4%]). Detection is uneven by attack type (Table 6): 9 of 13 types at 100%, but typosquatting 50% (4/8) and dependency confusion 0% (0/8), which the paper concedes require external registry metadata beyond local static analysis; DNS exfiltration 88.9% (16/18) and prompt injection 91.7% (22/24) also miss. By format (Table 5) Claude Code and OpenClaw skills hit F1=100% while MCP-server configs lag at recall 82.22%/F1 90.24%. Adding information-flow analysis to pattern matching yields only +3.0% coverage (8 extra multi-step A7 exfiltration skills; E3). SAT dependency resolution scales to 1,000 nodes in 92.4 ms (E4) and a full 540-skill scan takes 1.378 s / 2.55 ms per skill (E7). The 0% FP claim is framed as following from the soundness theorem rather than being merely empirical.
core_contribution: SkillFortify, presented as the first formal-analysis framework for the agent-skill supply chain (single-author, independent research). Rather than heuristic scanning, it aims to prove that an installed third-party agent skill cannot exceed its declared capabilities. Six paper-owned contributions: (1) DY-Skill, a Dolev-Yao adaptation to a five-phase skill lifecycle (Install, Load, Configure, Execute, Persist) with a claimed maximality proof (Thm 3.6); (2) a sound static analyzer built on abstract interpretation over a four-element access-level lattice {NONE<=READ<=WRITE<=ADMIN} across an 8-element resource universe, with a soundness theorem (Thm 4.9: no reported violation => no concrete access beyond declared capability); (3) object-capability-based sandboxing with a static confinement proof (Thm 5.6) and a runtime-enforcement design theorem left as future work (Thm 5.7); (4) an Agent Dependency Graph with SAT-encoded resolution plus a deterministic lockfile (Thm 6.10); (5) a multi-signal trust-score algebra with a monotonicity proof (Thm 7.12) and exponential decay for abandoned skills; and (6) SkillFortifyBench, a 540-skill benchmark and CLI tool (scan/sbom/lock). The distinguishing claim vs. prior tools is a machine-checkable soundness guarantee (zero false negatives on the analyzed properties), contrasted with heuristic scanners whose docs admit 'no findings does not mean no risk.'
threat_model: Adversary is the DY-Skill attacker: an author/registry/supply-chain/insider adversary who fully controls the skill 'network' (the supply chain SC = authors, registries, developers, execution environments) and can intercept, inject, modify, drop, forge skills (declare fewer capabilities than the code actually uses, caps_declared subset of caps_actual), and compromise registries (bounded only by unforgeable crypto signatures). Six attack classes over 10 (phase, class) attack surfaces: data exfiltration, privilege escalation, prompt injection, dependency confusion, typosquatting, namespace squatting. Four threat actors (malicious author, compromised registry, transitive-dependency poisoner, insider). Key assumption: a 'perfect sandbox' (analogous to perfect cryptography) that cannot be broken without an explicit escape capability. Motivating real-world incidents cited (not the paper's own findings): CVE-2026-25253 RCE in the OpenClaw runtime, the ClawHavoc campaign (1,200+ malicious skills), MalTool (6,487 malicious tools), and an empirical study finding 26.1% of 42,447 skills vulnerable.
limitations: 1) No head-to-head baseline: the paper explicitly does NOT run Snyk agent-scan, Cisco skill-scanner, or ToolShield on its benchmark (Sec 11.2); comparison is a qualitative feature table (Table 1) plus each tool's self-reported numbers. So the 96.95% F1 is not shown to beat any competing tool on equal footing. 2) Benchmark validity: all 540 skills are synthetically/deterministically generated with single-type attack patterns and constructive labels; the authors admit real malicious skills combine techniques/obfuscation/social engineering and that 94.07% recall is 'a lower bound on content-analyzable attacks.' The 100% precision / 0% FP is partly an artifact of benign skills being clean by construction. 3) The strongest guarantees are conditional/partial: runtime capability confinement (Thm 5.7) is a design-only 'future work' theorem -- v1 delivers only the static guarantee (Thm 5.6); code signing is deferred to V2; registry-dependent attacks (typosquatting, dependency confusion) are 0-50% and out of scope. 4) Soundness holds only over statically observable patterns; dynamically constructed URLs/shell commands need dynamic analysis the tool lacks. 5) Proofs are sketches with full versions relegated to appendices, and the whole ecosystem framing (OpenClaw, ClawHavoc, CVE-2026-25253) rests on very recent, single-author-curated incident citations.

datasets_used: SkillFortifyBench (paper's own): 540 agent skills, 270 malicious / 270 benign, evenly split across 180 Claude Code, 180 MCP-server, 180 OpenClaw manifests; 13 attack types (A1-A13); deterministically generated via seeded RNG for reproducibility
Synthetic dependency graphs of 10 to 1,000 nodes with randomized version constraints (for E4 SAT-resolution scalability)

Incident/prior-work corpora cited as motivation (NOT re-analyzed by this paper): MalTool (6,487 malicious tools), ClawHavoc campaign (1,200+ malicious skills), 'Agent Skills in the Wild' (42,447 skills, 26.1% vulnerable), CVE-2026-25253 advisory
quantitative_results: Per-format (Table 5): Claude Code F1 100%, OpenClaw F1 100%, MCP-server recall 82.22% / F1 90.24%
Per-attack-type detection (Table 6): 9/13 types at 100%; DNS exfiltration 88.9% (16/18), prompt injection 91.7% (22/24), typosquatting 50% (4/8), dependency confusion 0% (0/8)
E3 detection-method coverage (n=270 malicious): pattern-match only 246 (91.1%), pattern+info-flow 8 (3.0%), not detected 16 (5.9%); combined analysis adds only +3.0% over pattern matching alone
E4 SAT dependency resolution: 1,000 nodes in 92.4 ms (0.09 ms/skill); 200-500 nodes under 30 ms
E7 end-to-end: full 540-skill scan 1.378 s (2.55 ms/skill); sbom 0.007 s; lock 0.003 s

E5 trust algebra: monotonicity held on all 200 data points; weight-sensitivity mean CV 0.18 (0.07-0.31); abandoned-skill decay half-life ~139 days (lambda=0.005/day), 0.85 -> 0.52 at 90d -> 0.31 at 180d
E6 lockfile: byte-identical output across repeated runs on all 10 configurations (100% deterministic)
Attack-surface formalization: |resource universe|=8, 6 attack classes, |S_attack|=10 (phase,class) pairs
baselines_compared: No head-to-head evaluation on the shared benchmark (paper explicitly declines, Sec 11.2)

Qualitative feature comparison only (Table 1): Snyk agent-scan (~1,500 stars), Cisco skill-scanner (994 stars), ToolShield, ICSE-NIER '26 vision paper, mcp-audit, MCPShield -- SkillFortify claimed as only one with formal model + dep graph + lockfile + trust score + SBOM
Positioned against tools' self-reported metrics: Snyk 90-100% recall with 0% FPR on 100 benign skills; ToolShield 30% attack-success-rate reduction
Internal ablation (E3): pattern-matching-only vs pattern-matching + information-flow analysis
